en.extract:
The grey-crowned goldfinch (Carduelis caniceps), also known as the eastern goldfinch, Himalayan goldfinch or the grey-capped goldfinch, is a small passerine bird in the family Fringillidae that is distributed throughout Central Asia and the Himalayas.


Etymology
The genus name Carduelis comes from Linnaeus's specific epithet for the European Goldfinch Fringilla carduelis. Carduelis is the Latin name for the goldfinch.
The epithet caniceps is derived from the Latin canus (grey) and caput (head).


Phylogeny
The phylogenetic relationships among the Carduelis species are shown below, based on the data by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology.

The grey-crowned goldfinch separated from the European goldfinch about 1.5 million years ago, in the Quaternary period.


Taxonomy
Although first described by Vigors as a distinct species, the grey-crowned goldfinch was until recently widely treated as a subspecies group in the European goldfinch, primarily because it hybridised with it in southwestern Siberia and northern Iran. In 2016, BirdLife International restored its species status, and in 2024, the IOC World Bird List followed suit, accepting it as a separate species because of its very distinct plumage, lacking the black crown and vertical line behind the ear coverts of its relative.


Subspecies
Four subspecies are currently accepted:

Carduelis caniceps paropanisi Kollibay, 1910 (Western Turkmenistan and eastern Iran to northwest China) - paler face than C. c. caniceps and breast is almost entirely grey 
Carduelis caniceps subulata Gloger, 1833 (Northeast Kazakhstan, Siberia and Mongolia) - largest subspecies, upperparts pale buffish grey or tinged with cinnamon
Carduelis caniceps caniceps Vigors, 1831 (Western and northern Pakistan and northwestern Himalayas east to central Nepal) - smaller and darker grey than C. c. subulata
Carduelis caniceps ultima Koelz, 1949 - southern Iran (often considered to be conspecific with C. c. paropanisi) - longer billed than C. c. paropanisi


Description
The grey-crowned goldfinch is 10.5–14 cm (4.1–5.5 in) long with a wingspan of 21.5–25.5 cm (8.5–10.0 in). They weigh about 15–21 g (0.53–0.74 oz). 

The male has a brighter and more extensive red on the face and brighter yellow on the wing than the female, which appears to be the only notable difference between the sexes. Both sexes have a pinkish bill.

The juvenile lacks the red in the face, has faint streaking on breast and has buffish tips to coverts and tertial markings. The juvenile has duller and darker legs compared to the adult's pink to dull brown legs.


Vocalizations
The song of the grey-crowned goldfinch is a rather chaffinch-like combination, with a long mix of pleasant bubbling and twittering, though it lacks the "tinkling" notes of the European goldfinch. Its calls include twittering "deedelit" and "chirik", which do have a similar tinkling sound.


Distribution
The grey-crowned goldfinch breeds in southern and eastern Iran east through Afghanistan to the western Himalaya in northern and western Pakistan, northwestern India, and western Nepal, and northeast through Central Asia to the far west of Xinjiang in China. In the nonbreeding season it is found to lower levels in the Himalaya and Central Asia; in the Himalaya, it is a altitudinal migrant, breeding at 2,400–4,200 m altitude (rarely down to 1,500 m), and descending in winter to 1,900–2,400 m (rarely down to 75 m) altitude; similar altitudinal migration also occurs in Kazakhstan.
It occupies open and sparse deciduous woodland, mixed deciduous and conifer woods, forest edges at an altitude of 2100-3600 m, orchards and often parks and gardens. It usually lives at altitudes higher than the European goldfinch.


Vagrancy
The species has been recorded as a vagrant in Beijing, China. A vagrant, possibly C.carduelis subulata, was recorded in Japan.
Escaped or released captive birds have been reported in North America, but much more rarely than European goldfinch, with fewer than 1% of the number of that species. 


Behaviour and ecology


Flocks
They gather in flocks of four to several dozen birds and sometimes with other finches. They usually forage on the ground. They form flocks during autumn and winter to forage for food.


Diet
The diet consists mainly of seeds of various flowers, such as thistles, sunflowers, zinnia, chinar and dandelions. They are also known to eat arthropods.


Breeding
Their breeding season occurs from April to August. They nest in groups with up to five nests in one tree.
The birds have their first brood in May usually in pine trees between 1700 and 2100 meters. After this first brood, they travel higher to 3000-3300 meters in July and they nest in pines, willows, fruit trees in orchards and birches.
Nests, which are constructed by the female (although the male might assist in collection of material) are neatly constructed with mosses and grasses in the shape of a cup. Nests are located as high as 18m or higher. It lays 4 to 5 bluish-white eggs speckled with red and brown. The eggs, which are similar to those of European goldfinch, are about 1.8 cm × 1.3 cm (0.71 in × 0.51 in).  Cooperative breeding is not known in this species.
The eggs are incubated by the female for 9-12 days; the chicks stay in the nest for 13-18 days after hatching, and are fed by the parents for up to 10 days after leaving the nest.


Hybridization
The grey-crowned goldfinch has been known to hybridize with C.carduelis frigoris (with C.caniceps subulata in Siberia) and with C.carduelis brevirostris (with C.caniceps paropanisi, in Iran).


Gallery

The '''grey-crowned goldfinch''' ('''''Carduelis caniceps'''''), also known as the '''eastern goldfinch''', '''Himalayan goldfinch''' or the '''grey-capped goldfinch''', is a small [[passerine]] bird in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Fringillidae]] that is distributed throughout [[Central Asia]] and the [[Himalayas]].<ref name="Kirwan">{{Cite journal |last1=Kirwan |first1=Guy M. |last2=Moura |first2=Nárgila |last3=Clement |first3=Peter |last4=Pyle |first4=Peter |last5=Boesman |first5=Peter F. D. |date=2024 |title=Grey-crowned Goldfinch (Carduelis caniceps), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/eurgol2/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.eurgol2.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=BirdLife DataZone |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/grey-capped-goldfinch-carduelis-caniceps |access-date=2025-03-07 |website=datazone.birdlife.org |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Gray-crowned Goldfinch (Carduelis caniceps) |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1584584-Carduelis-caniceps |access-date=2025-04-04 |website=iNaturalist |language=en}}</ref>

== Etymology ==
The genus name ''Carduelis'' comes from [[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]]'s [[Specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] for the [[European goldfinch|European Goldfinch]] ''Fringilla carduelis''. ''Carduelis'' is the Latin name for the goldfinch.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Paynter |first=Raymond A. Jnr. |title=Check-list of the birds of the world, Volume 14 |publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology |year=1968 |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts |pages=234 |language=English}}</ref>

The epithet ''caniceps'' is derived from the Latin ''canus'' (grey) and ''caput'' (head).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names: From Aalge to Zusii |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |edition=1. Aufl. |location=London}}</ref>

== Phylogeny ==
{{Phylogeny/Carduelis}}The grey-crowned goldfinch separated from the [[European goldfinch]] about 1.5 million years ago, in the [[Quaternary]] period.

== Taxonomy ==
Although first described by [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors]] as a distinct species,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Zoological Society of London. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/46215 |title=Proceedings of the Committee of Science and Correspondence of the Zoological Society of London |last2=London |first2=Zoological Society of |last3=London |first3=Zoological Society of |date=1830 |publisher=Printed for the Society by Richard Taylor |volume=pt.1-2 (1830-1832) |location=[London]}}</ref> the grey-crowned goldfinch was until recently widely treated as a [[subspecies]] group in the [[European goldfinch]], primarily because it [[hybrid (biology)|hybridised]] with it in southwestern Siberia and northern Iran. In 2016, [[BirdLife International]] restored its species status, and in 2024, the [[IOC World Bird List]] followed suit, accepting it as a separate species because of its very distinct plumage, lacking the black crown and vertical line behind the ear coverts of its relative.<ref name="Kirwan" />

=== Subspecies ===
Four subspecies are currently accepted:<ref>{{Cite web |title=Finches, euphonias, longspurs, Thrush-tanager – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/finches/ |access-date=2025-03-07 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org}}</ref>
* ''Carduelis caniceps paropanisi'' [[Paul Robert Kollibay|Kollibay]], 1910 (Western [[Turkmenistan]] and eastern [[Iran]] to northwest [[China]]) - paler face than ''C. c. caniceps'' and breast is almost entirely grey <ref>{{Cite book |last=Clement |first=Peter |title=Finches and Sparrows |date=2011 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing Plc |others=John Davis, Alan Harris |isbn=978-1-4081-3508-2 |series=Helm Identification Guides |location=London}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Hoyo |first=Josep del |title=Handbook of the birds of the world |last2=Elliott |first2=Andrew |last3=Christie |first3=David A. |last4=Bock |first4=Walter Joseph |last5=Collar |first5=Nigel James |date=2010 |publisher=Lynx ed |isbn=978-84-96553-68-2 |location=Barcelona}}</ref>
* ''Carduelis caniceps subulata'' [[C. L. Gloger|Gloger]], 1833 (Northeast [[Kazakhstan]], [[Siberia]] and [[Mongolia]]) - largest subspecies, upperparts pale buffish grey or tinged with cinnamon<ref name=":1" />
* ''Carduelis caniceps caniceps'' [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors]], 1831 (Western and northern [[Pakistan]] and northwestern [[Himalayas]] east to central [[Nepal]]) - smaller and darker grey than ''C. c. subulata''<ref name=":1" />
* ''Carduelis caniceps ultima'' [[Walter Koelz|Koelz]], 1949 - southern [[Iran]] (often considered to be conspecific with ''C. c. paropanisi'') - longer billed than ''C. c. paropanisi''<ref name="Kirwan" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Vaurie |first=Charles |url=http://archive.org/details/notesonsomeasia1424vaur |title=Notes on some Asiatic finches |date=1949 |publisher=New York : American Museum of Natural History |others=American Museum of Natural History Library}}</ref>

== Description ==
The grey-crowned goldfinch is {{cvt|10.5|–|14|cm}} long with a wingspan of {{cvt|21.5|–|25.5|cm}}.<ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book | last=Hoyo | first=Josep del | title=All the Birds of the World | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=2020 | isbn=978-84-16728-37-4 | page=768}}</ref><ref name="Kazmierczak">{{cite book | last1=Kazmierczak | first1=Krys | last2=Perlo | first2=Ber van | title=A field guide to the birds of the Indian subcontinent | publisher=Yale University Press | publication-place=New Haven | date=2000 | isbn=0-300-07921-4 | page=300}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Cramp |first=Stanley |title=Crows to finches |date=1994 |publisher=Oxford Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-19-854679-5 |editor-last=Cramp |editor-first=Stanley |series=Handbook of the birds of Europe, the Middle East and North Africa : the birds of the Western Palearctic / Stanley Cramp (Ed.) |location=Oxford}}</ref> They weigh about {{cvt|15|-|21|g}}. 
[[File:Carduelis caniceps - Анатолий Озерной Anatoly Ozernoy - 593924608.jpeg|left|thumb|244x244px|Feathers]]
The male has a brighter and more extensive red on the face and brighter yellow on the wing<ref>{{Cite book |last=Blanford |first=W. T. |title=The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma |date=1890}}</ref> than the female, which appears to be the only notable difference between the sexes.<ref name="Kirwan" /> Both sexes have a pinkish bill.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Gray-crowned Goldfinch - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/eurgol2 |access-date=2025-04-04 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref>
[[File:Carduelis caniceps - Михаил Голомысов - 562161771.jpeg|thumb|Juvenile in [[Russia]]]]
The juvenile lacks the red in the face, has faint streaking on breast and has buffish tips to coverts and tertial markings.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Grimmett |first1=Richard |title=Birds of the Indian Subcontinent |last2=Inskipp |first2=Carol |last3=Inskipp |first3=Tim |publisher=[[Christopher Helm]] |year=2016 |isbn=978-81-93315-09-5 |edition=2nd |pages=476–477 |language=English}}</ref> The juvenile has duller and darker legs compared to the adult's pink to dull brown legs.<ref name="Kirwan" />

== Vocalizations ==
The song of the grey-crowned goldfinch is a rather chaffinch-like combination, with a long mix of pleasant bubbling and twittering, though it lacks the "tinkling" notes of the European goldfinch. Its calls include twittering "deedelit" and "chirik", which do have a similar tinkling sound.<ref name="Kazmierczak" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Birds of the World |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/ornith-notes/JN100356 |access-date=2026-02-16 |website=birdsoftheworld.org |language=en}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
The grey-crowned goldfinch breeds in southern and eastern [[Iran]] east through [[Afghanistan]] to the western [[Himalaya]] in northern and western [[Pakistan]], northwestern [[India]], and western [[Nepal]], and northeast through [[Central Asia]] to the far west of [[Xinjiang]] in [[China]]. In the nonbreeding season it is found to lower levels in the Himalaya and Central Asia; in the Himalaya, it is a [[bird migration|altitudinal migrant]], breeding at 2,400–4,200 m altitude (rarely down to 1,500 m), and descending in winter to 1,900–2,400 m (rarely down to 75 m) altitude;<ref name="Kazmierczak"/> similar altitudinal migration also occurs in Kazakhstan.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Wassink |first1=Arend |title=The Birds of Kazakhstan |last2=Oreel |first2=G.J. |year=2007 |publisher=Arend Wassink |isbn=978-90-81146-21-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=http://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0015unse |title=Handbook of the birds of the world |date=1992 |publisher=Barcelona : Lynx Edicions |others=Internet Archive |isbn=978-84-87334-10-8}}</ref>

It occupies open and sparse deciduous woodland, mixed deciduous and conifer woods, forest edges at an altitude of 2100-3600 m,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Grimmett |first=Richard |title=Birds of Pakistan |date=2008 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing Plc |others=Tim Inskipp |isbn=978-0-7136-8800-9 |series=Helm Field Guides |location=London}}</ref> orchards and often parks and gardens.<ref name="Kirwan" /> It usually lives at altitudes higher than the [[European goldfinch]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Ayé |first1=Raffael |title=Birds of Central Asia |last2=Schweizer |first2=Manuel |last3=Roth |first3=Tobias |date=2012 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing PLC |isbn=978-0-7136-7038-7 |series=Helm Field Guides |location=London}}</ref>

=== Vagrancy ===
The species has been recorded as a [[vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in [[Beijing]], China.<ref>{{Cite web |last=su |first=peng |date=2023-12-05 |title=eBird Checklist - 5 Dec 2023 - 绿堤公园 (Green Bank Park) - 37 species |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/checklist/S155775669 |access-date=2025-03-10 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> A vagrant, possibly ''C.carduelis subulata'', was recorded in [[Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Birds of Japan|last=Chikara|first=Otani|date=2019|publisher=Lynx Edicions|isbn=9788416728121}}</ref>

Escaped or released captive birds have been reported in [[North America]], but much more rarely than [[European goldfinch]], with fewer than 1% of the number of that species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Craves |first1=Julie A. |last2=Anich |first2=Nicholas M. |date=2023-01-24 |title=Status and distribution of an introduced population of European Goldfinches (Carduelis carduelis) in the western Great Lakes region of North America |url=https://neobiota.pensoft.net/article/97736/ |journal=NeoBiota |volume=81 |pages=129–155 |doi=10.3897/neobiota.81.97736 |doi-access=free |issn=1314-2488}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=McGrath |first=Steven |date=2020-02-04 |title=Gray-crowned Goldfinch (Carduelis caniceps) |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/38305335 |access-date=2025-04-04 |website=iNaturalist |language=en}}</ref> 

== Behaviour and ecology ==

=== Flocks ===
They gather in flocks of four to several dozen birds and sometimes with other finches. They usually forage on the ground.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Grewal |first1=Bikram |title=A Naturalist's Guide to the Birds of India, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka |last2=Bhatia |first2=Garima |publisher=[[John Beaufoy Publishing]] |year=2014 |isbn=9788172345389 |edition=1st |pages=147 |language=English}}</ref> They form flocks during autumn and winter to forage for food.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Grey-crowned Goldfinch (Carduelis caniceps), Gilgit (52573085360).jpg|thumb|Feeding on sunflower seeds]]

=== Diet ===
The diet consists mainly of seeds of various flowers, such as [[thistle]]s, [[Common sunflower|sunflowers]], [[zinnia]], [[Platanus orientalis|chinar]] and [[Taraxacum|dandelions]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Ali |first=Salim |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan: Together with Those of Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan and Sri Lanka |last2=Ripley |first2=Sidney Dillon |date=December 1999 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |edition=2nd |volume=10}}</ref> They are also known to eat [[arthropod]]s.<ref name="Kirwan" />

=== Breeding ===
Their breeding season occurs from April to August. They nest in groups with up to five nests in one tree.

The birds have their first brood in May usually in pine trees between 1700 and 2100 meters. After this first brood, they travel higher to 3000-3300 meters in July and they nest in pines, willows, fruit trees in orchards<ref>{{Cite book |last=R S P  Bates |url=http://archive.org/details/biostor-147674 |title=The Nesting Habits of the Goldfinch Carduelis Carduelis (Linn.) and C. Caniceps Vigors |date=1960 |language=English}}</ref> and birches.<ref>{{Cite book |last=B B Osmaston |url=http://archive.org/details/biostor-150616 |title=Notes on the Birds of Kashmir |date=1927}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Grimmett |first=Richard |title=Birds of Northern India |last2=Inskipp |first2=Tim |publisher=Christopher Helm Publications |year=2017 |isbn=9789388271110 |pages=272-273}}</ref>

Nests, which are constructed by the female (although the male might assist in collection of material) are neatly constructed with mosses and grasses in the shape of a cup. Nests are located as high as 18m or higher.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Bates |first1=R. S. P. |title=Breeding Birds of Kashmir |last2=Lowther |first2=E. H. N. |date=December 1991 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780195625622 |language=English}}</ref><ref name="Kirwan" /> It lays 4 to 5 bluish-white eggs speckled with red and brown. The eggs, which are similar to those of European goldfinch, are about {{cvt|1.8|×|1.3|cm}}. <ref>{{Cite book |last=Dresser |first=H. E. |title=A History of the Birds of Europe Including All the Species Inhabiting the Western Palearctic Region |year=1881 |volume=1}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> Cooperative breeding is not known in this species.<ref name="Kirwan" />

The eggs are incubated by the female for 9-12 days; the chicks stay in the nest for 13-18 days after hatching, and are fed by the parents for up to 10 days after leaving the nest.<ref name=":1" />

=== Hybridization ===
The grey-crowned goldfinch has been known to hybridize with ''C.carduelis frigoris'' (with ''C.caniceps subulata'' in [[Siberia]]) and with ''C.carduelis brevirostris'' (with ''C.caniceps paropanisi'', in [[Iran]]).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Clement |first=Peter |title=Finches & sparrows |last2=Harris |first2=Alan |date=1999 |publisher=Princeton Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-691-04878-9 |edition=Pb.- Ausg |location=Princeton}}</ref>
